text: From Newcastle Hospital, this is Ahmed Khabib. I'd like to present to you this fifty-year-old lady that had progressive chest infections and over the last twelve months started to complain of neutropenic sepsis with frequent admissions into the intensive care unit. She was worked up and found to have thymoma in the anterior mediastinum.

The surgeon uses three ports on the left side, including a submemory incision and one in the axilla. The mediastinal pleura is opened anterior to the left phrenic nerve, revealing a lump on the left thymic lobe. The surgeon dissects around the lump inferiorly, lifting it and opening the capsule off the fibrous pericardium to release the mass.

The surgeon dissects the thymic mass from underneath, carefully lifting it off the pulmonary trunk and the ascending and arch of the aorta. The dissection reveals the blood vessels, including the vein and artery draining into and out of the left internal mammary, while taking care to avoid injury to the left phrenic nerve.

The surgeon observes the aggressive and invasive appearance of the tumor. They proceed to cautiously work on the innominate vein, establishing a surgical plane. Retraction is applied to the left thymic horn, which extends towards the neck, following its path up to the thyroid gland.

The surgeon uses a hook to support the thymic tissue and employs a bipolar fenestrated instrument to drag down the tissue. The vessels at the top of the thymic horn, originating from the thyroid, are identified and clipped for safety before using diathermy to divide the tissue.
The surgeon releases tissue on the right side, approaching the confluence of the innominate veins into the superior vena cava (SVC).

The surgeon carefully dissects around large vessels using blunt dissection, applying Hemolock clips to isolate the vein draining into the superior vena cava (SVC). The right phrenic nerve is visible, descending from the right internal mammary base onto the lateral side of the SVC, and is carefully avoided during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node and uses a bipolar device to safely divide it without conducting electricity to the phrenic nerve. The right pleura is opened to enhance visualization, and the final portion is divided. An endocatch bag is used to prevent sealing in the left pleura.

The surgeon inserts instruments through a twelve millimeter port in the axilla, closes the endocatch bag, and pulls it through. The innominate vein and aorta are visibly clear of any surrounding tissue, with a comparison image showing the area before and after tumor resection.
Thank you for watching. This is Ahmed Habib from New Cross Hospital.
